quotes.quote: Mono itself is a fantastic piece of software and one of the best programming environments Linux users got since ages.
quotes.summary: Ronacher argues that Mono is an excellent, underappreciated development environment for Linux, and that much of the hostility toward it stems from politics and fear rather than technical merit. He acknowledges common criticisms around Microsoft, Novell, and patents, but says they don't make Mono itself a bad choice. He praises C# and Mono’s modern language features, library consistency, and multi-language runtime, contrasting them favorably with Java and even Python in some areas. He concedes documentation gaps and that Mono may lag Microsoft, yet insists that it already delivers the features developers actually need. The conclusion is a direct call to ignore the FUD and try Mono based on firsthand experience.
quotes.key_insight: Mono’s technical merits and C#’s language design outweigh the political and patent fears that dominate the conversation.

He praises C# and Mono’s modern language features, library consistency, and multi-language runtime, contrasting them favorably with Java and even Python in some areas. He concedes documentation gaps and that Mono may lag Microsoft, yet insists that it already delivers the features developers actually need. The conclusion is a direct call to ignore the FUD and try Mono based on firsthand experience.
quotes.key_insight: Mono’s technical merits and C#’s language design outweigh the political and patent fears that dominate the conversation.

quotes.quote: Every once in a while I'm really impressed by a library I stumble upon.
quotes.summary: Ronacher recounts discovering Fabric as a more pleasant, Pythonic alternative to Capistrano for deploying web apps. He argues that combining Fabric with a custom virtualenv bootstrap makes deployment simple and repeatable. With a couple of Fabric commands, servers can be provisioned, dependencies built, and apps updated. He highlights Fabric’s broader utility beyond Python because it just uses SSH. He concludes that Fabric could also replace Makefiles for common project tasks, with the bonus of working on Windows.
quotes.key_insight: Fabric’s Pythonic, SSH-driven workflow makes deployment and automation simpler and more portable than the existing Ruby-based tooling.

quotes.quote: Python doesn't care about Whitespace at all.
quotes.summary: Ronacher argues that Python is often mislabeled as “whitespace sensitive,” but its parser only consumes indentation into tokens and then ignores whitespace. He contrasts that with Ruby, where spaces can change the parse tree and semantics. He shows that `foo[42]` and `foo [42]` are distinct in Ruby, affecting whether `foo` is called with an array or its return value is indexed. A second example demonstrates how a tiny space can flip division into an unterminated regex parse error. He concludes that Ruby, not Python, exhibits the more troublesome whitespace sensitivity, largely due to Ruby’s parser resolving ambiguities based on local variable tracking.
quotes.key_insight: Python’s whitespace complaints are overstated; Ruby’s parsing rules make it far more sensitive to spaces in practice.

quotes.quote: It's time to set the record straight, and prove once and for all that the Pythonistas are wrong.
quotes.summary: Ronacher argues that the Python community consistently misinterprets the name “Python” as referring to snakes. He insists the language is named after Monty Python, not reptiles, and uses this to critique the prevalence of snake-themed logos across the ecosystem. He cites official and community logos (Python, SciPy, PyCon, lxml) as evidence of a widespread branding mistake. The post is a tongue-in-cheek but pointed correction: the name is cultural, not zoological. He concludes that many projects have “the wrong logo,” and the community should recognize the Monty Python origin.
quotes.key_insight: Branding in tech should reflect origin and culture, and in Python’s case the Monty Python roots are repeatedly ignored in favor of incorrect snake imagery.

quotes.quote: OS X is just another proprietary operating system, and not the solution for all of your problems.
quotes.summary: Ronacher argues that the Rails community’s push toward OS X and GitHub reflects a closed, elitist posture rather than genuine productivity gains. He challenges the assumption that OS X is inherently superior to Windows, pointing out tradeoffs in hardware cost, editor ergonomics, and inconsistent UX. He details practical frustrations with OS X—package management gaps, confusing app bundles, Finder limitations, mouse behavior, and performance issues. He also notes OS X’s security update burden and its confusing Unix layer compared to Linux and Windows. The conclusion is that every OS has serious flaws, OS X is not a universal solution, and judging developers by their OS is lazy and ignorant.
quotes.key_insight: OS X is not inherently better for developers; it has its own serious flaws, and OS choice should not be a proxy for competence.

quotes.quote: I would compare progressive metal more with Jazz and classical music than with thrash metal or death metal.
quotes.summary: Ronacher argues that progressive metal stands apart from other metal because its concept albums, technical compositions, and narrative scope make it closer to jazz or classical than to thrash or death metal. He explains that the genre is rarely on radio because songs are long and only fully make sense within full-album context. He frames progressive metal as music that demands focused listening, like reading a book. To illustrate, he walks through several favorite albums and their stories or structural concepts. He concludes by encouraging metal fans who have not tried progressive metal to give it a chance.
quotes.key_insight: Progressive metal rewards deep, album-length listening through technical composition and narrative ambition, which is why it feels more like literature or classical music than mainstream metal.

quotes.quote: It has XML syntax, XML namespace declarations but what doesn't it have? A doctype.
quotes.summary: Ronacher argues that WordPress’s WXR export is labeled XML but violates basic XML expectations in ways that make real XML tooling unusable. He describes missing doctypes, reliance on HTML entities, and unescaped HTML in comments that cause standard parsers to fail. WordPress’s own importer works only because it ignores XML correctness and uses regex, which breaks namespace handling and proper escaping. The result is that compliant XML output is incompatible with WordPress, forcing developers to mirror the flaws. He concludes with a bitter loss of faith in standards and the project’s claims about web standards.
quotes.key_insight: A format that claims to be XML but breaks core rules forces developers to abandon standard tooling and erodes trust in the platform’s stated commitment to standards.

quotes.quote: Because comments are neither escaped nor marked as CDATA.
quotes.summary: Ronacher argues that WordPress’s WXR export is labeled XML but violates basic XML expectations in ways that make real XML tooling unusable. He describes missing doctypes, reliance on HTML entities, and unescaped HTML in comments that cause standard parsers to fail. WordPress’s own importer works only because it ignores XML correctness and uses regex, which breaks namespace handling and proper escaping. The result is that compliant XML output is incompatible with WordPress, forcing developers to mirror the flaws. He concludes with a bitter loss of faith in standards and the project’s claims about web standards.
quotes.key_insight: A format that claims to be XML but breaks core rules forces developers to abandon standard tooling and erodes trust in the platform’s stated commitment to standards.

quotes.quote: Subversion works like FTP whereas mercurial is bittorrent.
quotes.summary: The post argues that Mercurial’s distributed model is fundamentally different from Subversion’s centralized, server‑dependent workflow. Ronacher explains that in Mercurial there is no special server, every clone is a full repository, and branching/merging are first‑class operations rather than painful exceptions. He walks through how cloning, pulling, updating, and merging work locally, and how configuration is stored in per‑repo `hgrc`. He emphasizes that contributions can flow through bundles, patchbombs, or ad‑hoc servers even without push access. The conclusion is pragmatic: Mercurial enables fast, resilient collaboration and makes it easy to keep history intact across contributors.
quotes.key_insight: Mercurial’s distributed design removes the server bottleneck, making branching, collaboration, and offline continuity natural rather than exceptional.

quotes.quote: All three template engines are totally different but have a one thing in common: All three are the "second generation" of template engines.
quotes.summary: Ronacher compares Mako, Jinja, and Genshi as second-generation Python template engines with distinct philosophies and tradeoffs. He explains how his own needs, including WSGI routing, dynamic inheritance, and performance, led him to use Mako for logic-heavy templates. He contrasts that with Jinja’s designer-friendly, Django-inspired syntax and safer, more expressive template language. He then positions Genshi as a powerful XML-stream engine with automatic escaping and transformation features but a steeper learning curve and slower performance. He concludes that the variety is a strength and that different contexts justify different engines.
quotes.key_insight: Template engines embody different tradeoffs, so choosing among Mako, Jinja, and Genshi should be driven by the project’s workflow, audience, and performance needs.

quotes.quote: Open Source software often takes some time until a 1.0 is released and that's perfectly fine.
quotes.summary: Ronacher argues that calling Django 1.0 “2.0” is a marketing move that misleads about maturity. He says version numbers should reflect substance, and Django still trails Rails in key integrated features like migrations, REST, and debugging. Django shines when you stay within its intended use cases, but becomes painful once you push beyond them. He details ORM and admin limitations that force hacks, replacements, and eventually abandonment of core contrib modules. The conclusion is that Django is valuable but not ready to be labeled 2.0.
quotes.key_insight: Django is strong within its intended scope, but its real-world scaling and extensibility gaps make a 2.0 label premature.

quotes.quote: And of course also why it sucks a lot less than any other language.
quotes.summary: Ronacher delivers a tongue-in-cheek rant listing concrete design and library grievances with Python, from naming conventions and stdlib structure to missing language features. He returns repeatedly to the need to "clean up the stdlib" and calls out inconsistency, poor module layout, and awkward APIs. The complaints mix stylistic frustrations (naming, flat stdlib) with deeper language wishes (blocks, assignment expressions, regex literals, Unicode unification). Despite the harsh title, he concludes Python "sucks less" than other languages due to strong metaprogramming, solid libraries, readable syntax, speed, bindings, and a friendly community. The post’s final stance is a critical but loyal endorsement: Python is flawed, yet still a pragmatic best-in-class choice.
quotes.key_insight: Python has real design and stdlib flaws, but its ecosystem, metaprogramming, and community make it the least-bad, most productive option.

quotes.quote: WSGI is lower level than CGI which you probably know.
quotes.summary: Ronacher introduces WSGI as a lower-level, scalable alternative to CGI that sits between web apps and servers and is defined by a simple Python callable interface. He walks through a basic WSGI app, then expands it with URL dispatching, environment usage, and middleware to show how routing and error handling can be built from first principles. He emphasizes that while it’s possible to build these pieces yourself, existing libraries like Werkzeug and Paste provide higher-level conveniences. He outlines deployment options and argues that WSGI enables flexible, composable stacks. The post concludes that WSGI is powerful and approachable, and that utility libraries can simplify the work without constraining you.
quotes.key_insight: WSGI’s simple callable interface makes web apps portable and composable, and you can either build the stack yourself or lean on utilities without losing flexibility.

The post concludes that WSGI is powerful and approachable, and that utility libraries can simplify the work without constraining you.
quotes.key_insight: WSGI’s simple callable interface makes web apps portable and composable, and you can either build the stack yourself or lean on utilities without losing flexibility.

quotes.quote: WSGI rocks. You can simply create your own personal stack.
quotes.summary: Ronacher introduces WSGI as a lower-level, scalable alternative to CGI that sits between web apps and servers and is defined by a simple Python callable interface. He walks through a basic WSGI app, then expands it with URL dispatching, environment usage, and middleware to show how routing and error handling can be built from first principles. He emphasizes that while it’s possible to build these pieces yourself, existing libraries like Werkzeug and Paste provide higher-level conveniences. He outlines deployment options and argues that WSGI enables flexible, composable stacks. The post concludes that WSGI is powerful and approachable, and that utility libraries can simplify the work without constraining you.

quotes.quote: I seldom read such a piece of crap.
quotes.summary: Ronacher delivers a harsh critique of Dan Brown’s "Digital Fortress," arguing that the novel is riddled with basic technical errors and shallow, sensationalized depictions of technology and security. He walks through the plot and then systematically dismantles its handling of computers, networking, and cryptography, stressing that the book misuses terminology and violates even elementary principles. He also objects to implausible cultural and geographic portrayals, particularly of Spain. The cumulative effect, in his view, is a story that collapses under its own factual mistakes. He concludes that the book is dull, childish, and not worth reading or buying.
quotes.key_insight: The novel’s central premise fails because it is built on fundamental misunderstandings of cryptography and computing.
